fact: The R101 was half a million cubic feet short of lift
source: To Ride the Storm: The Story of the Airship R.101, Sir Peter G. Masefield (1982); also documented in the Court of Inquiry findings

fact: Engineers sliced the R101 in half to add another gas bag
source: An extra bay (gas bag 'Bay 0') was inserted by cutting the ship in two and adding a new section, documented in the R101 Public Inquiry Report (1931)

fact: The outer fabric leaked hydrogen in rain
source: The outer cover's doped fabric was found to deteriorate and allow gas to escape, especially when wet — detailed in the Report of the R.101 Inquiry (Cmd. 3825, 1931)

fact: The R101 crashed in France while flying low in rain with its nose dipping
source: The R101 crashed near Beauvais, France on 5 October 1930 after losing altitude in bad weather. Report of the R.101 Inquiry (1931)
48 of 54 people on board perished in the crash and fire. Widely documented; confirmed in the official Court of Inquiry report and multiple sources.

fact: No one would tell the Air Minister (Lord Thomson) his airship wasn't ready to fly
source: Lord Thomson, Secretary of State for Air, pressured the schedule for a flight to India; engineers' concerns were not effectively communicated. Discussed in Masefield's 'To Ride the Storm' (1982) and Nevil Shute's 'Slide Rule' (1954)
